quotes.verbatim_quote:
The
F#tdim/7 chord is a substitute for the dominant VI7 chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the core harmonic function of the passing diminished chord, grounding what might seem like a chromatic anomaly in conventional dominant-substitution theory.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
F#dim7: F#-A-C-Eb
D7(b9): D-F#-A-C-Eb

Do you see the similarities in these two chords? They are virtually the
same except the root in the D7(b9). You can think of the F#dim7 as a
rootless D7(b9).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the student the concrete pitch-level proof that dim7 and dominant b9 share all tones except the root, making the substitution relationship audible and analyzable.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now, on a compositional level the F#dim7 is functioning as more than
just a substitution. It’s also a creating a chromatic passing note, in this
case the bass note, to connect the | chord with the ii chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distinguishes two simultaneous functions of the diminished chord—harmonic substitution and voice-leading bass motion—giving students a dual analytical framework.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The second element is the pattern being used. Yes, it’s a series of
ii-V-l’s which our ears recognize. But the key centers are moving in a
particular order. They are moving in 3rds.

Bb to Gb is a descending major 3" interval.
Gb to D is a descending major 3" interval.
D to Gb is an ascending major 3" interval.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the structural logic (cycle of major 3rds) behind the bridge's rapid key changes, directly linking this song's construction to Coltrane Changes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Luckily, when we break this bridge section down into key centers, it
begins to feel easier to understand. Instead of looking at it as a mish-
mash of seemingly unrelated chords, you can just look at it as a bunch
of ii-V-l’s in different key centers.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the practical navigational strategy for the bridge: reframe complex non-diatonic motion as a sequence of familiar ii-V-I units, each in its own momentary key.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It’s called the “Whole-Half” diminished because
the intervallic formula for this scale is: W-H-W-H-W-H-W. The scale is
based on alternating whole steps and half steps.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the Whole-Half diminished scale by its intervallic construction, giving students the theoretical basis for note choice over diminished 7 chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Remember that it is being substituted for the VI7b9 chord, so any of
those chord tones will serve you well.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a direct, actionable improv rule: treat the passing dim7 as a dominant b9 and target its chord tones, connecting harmony analysis to melodic practice.

quotes.topic: Connecting key centers by half/whole step
quotes.verbatim_quote:
It’s important to notice that | do my best to enter into each new key
center by half steps or whole steps. I’m referring to the first and last
notes of connecting bars. This isn’t a rule or mandatory by any means,
but it does allow for a smooth flow in the lines.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Teaches a concrete melodic technique for bridging rapid key changes—stepwise connection at bar boundaries—while correctly framing it as a preference rather than a rule.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It's also perfectly fine to not connected the key centers together. |
did this in bar 3 of this last lick. | ended on a half note and started a
new line in the next bar. Nothing wrong with that. As long as your line
doesn’t sound like it’s unnaturally starting and stopping, this can be a
great solution.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Validates the alternative approach of phrase separation at key-center changes, preventing students from over-applying the stepwise-connection technique.
